Title: Endoplasmic reticulum-derived bodies enable a single-cell chemical defense in Brassicaceae plants

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products; defense-related specialized metabolites
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant feeding assay referenced in results
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: nai1 mutant seedlings are more susceptible to woodlice, consistent with NAI1 regulating BGLU23 expression
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. nai1 seedlings are preferentially eaten by woodlice. 2. This supports that regulation of BGLU23 expression contributes to chemical defense.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a primary mechanistic focus of this paper; included because a mutant was directly tested in results, but its regulatory mechanism was not dissected here.

Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Woodlouse feeding assay in qKO mutant
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Quadruple mutant feeding assay referenced in results
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Part of qKO glucosinolate-deficient background that is strongly preferred by woodlice
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Loss of MYB28 together with MYB29, CYP79B2, and CYP79B3 causes glucosinolate deficiency. 2. qKO seedlings and roots are more susceptible to woodlouse feeding.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used only in a quadruple mutant context; not mechanistically analyzed in this paper.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Allium cepa; Nicotiana tabacum
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products; defense-related specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Chemical defense against herbivory; deterrence of woodlice
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glucosinolates are stored in vacuoles; toxic products are generated upon tissue/organelle damage in seedlings and roots
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Glucosinolate hydrolysis / mustard-oil bomb-related chemical defense
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Single-cell ER body–vacuole defense system in seedlings and roots
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glycoside hydrolase family 1 β-glucosidase; C-terminal ER-retention signal KDEL
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes endogenous glucosinolates after tissue damage to generate toxic defense compounds, inferred to include isothiocyanates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Various glucosinolates including aliphatic, aromatic, and indole glucosinolates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products; toxic compounds including isothiocyanates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly converts stored glucosinolates into toxic end products upon organellar damage
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key terminal determinant of the single-cell chemical defense, but not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly abundant in seedlings and roots; major component of ER bodies; absent/undetectable in seedlings for TGG1/TGG2 but BGLU23 abundant
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: In Arabidopsis seedlings and roots, accumulates in ER bodies, especially epidermal/root hair cells; when heterologously expressed in onion and tobacco, localizes to induced ER bodies with NAI2
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seedling stage; root tissues; tissue damage; woodlouse feeding; homogenate incubation at 26°C for 30 min; scopoletin treatment in tobacco cells; heterologous coexpression with NAI2
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Pro35S (constitutive) for BGLU23/BGLU23-GFP-KDEL in onion and tobacco; native NAI2 promoter used for NAI2-GFP lines
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: ER bodies; endoplasmic reticulum lumen; relocalizes to vacuole when NAI2 is absent
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: NAI2; PYK10-binding protein 1 (background, cited prior work)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolomics comparison of wild type vs bglu23 bglu21 before/after incubation; double mutant analysis; woodlouse dual-choice feeding assay; heterologous expression in onion and tobacco; GFP localization; immunoblotting; co-immunoprecipitation/mass spectrometry
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes hydrolysis of multiple endogenous glucosinolates and confers resistance to woodlice; loss of BGLU23/BGLU21 causes retention of glucosinolates in homogenates and increased herbivore feeding
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGLU23 is a major ER-body β-glucosidase in Arabidopsis seedlings and roots. 2. Together with BGLU21, it hydrolyzes at least 13 endogenous glucosinolates spanning aliphatic, aromatic, and indole classes. 3. Loss of BGLU23/BGLU21 strongly increases seedling susceptibility to woodlice. 4. Coexpression of BGLU23 with NAI2 is sufficient to induce ER bodies in non-Brassicaceae cells. 5. NAI2-dependent sequestration prevents BGLU23 leakage to vacuoles and degradation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Without ER-body sequestration, BGLU23 leaks into vacuoles and is degraded; premature reaction with vacuolar glucosides is a proposed risk
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics: MetaboLights MTBLS1383; Proteomics (interactor identification context): PRIDE PXD016606
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering Brassicaceae-like single-cell chemical defense when combined with NAI2; useful for pest resistance engineering, but requires sequestration strategy to avoid vacuolar leakage/degradation
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Artificial expression alone is insufficient to efficiently form ER bodies in non-Brassicaceae cells; KDEL contributes to ER retention and ER-body formation efficiency. The paper identifies endogenous substrates but does not biochemically enumerate all hydrolysis products in vivo.

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products; defense-related specialized metabolites
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Chemical defense against herbivory; deterrence of woodlice
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Glucosinolates stored in vacuoles of seedling/root cells
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Glucosinolate hydrolysis / mustard-oil bomb-related chemical defense
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Glycoside hydrolase family 1 β-glucosidase; ER-body-localized; C-terminal ER-retention signal KDEL
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyzes endogenous glucosinolates in concert with BGLU23 after tissue damage
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products; toxic compounds including isothiocyanates
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly converts stored glucosinolates into toxic defense products
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Contributory terminal enzyme; less abundant than BGLU23
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Localized to ER bodies; less abundant homolog of BGLU23 in seedlings
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detected functionally in Arabidopsis seedlings via bglu23 bglu21 double mutant
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seedling stage; tissue damage; woodlouse feeding; homogenate incubation at 26°C for 30 min
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Double mutant metabolomics and feeding assay
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Acts with BGLU23 to hydrolyze endogenous glucosinolates and support seedling defense against woodlice
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BGLU21 is an ER-body-localized homolog of BGLU23. 2. The bglu23 bglu21 double mutant fails to deplete multiple glucosinolates during homogenate incubation. 3. Loss of both enzymes causes strong susceptibility of seedlings to woodlice.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Its individual in vivo contribution was not separated from BGLU23 in this paper; described as less abundant than BGLU23.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Allium cepa; Nicotiana tabacum
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Class: Glucosinolate hydrolysis products; defense-related specialized metabolites
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Defense system operates in seedlings and roots where glucosinolates are spatially separated from BGLU23 until damage
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Brassicaceae-specific ER-body component with ~10 repeats of ~40-aa sequence containing acidic motif (Glu-Phe-Glu)
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: ER bodies; endoplasmic reticulum-associated luminal component
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with BGLU23 in an ER-body-associated complex or assembly
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Protein sequestration / organelle biogenesis control; stabilizes BGLU23 by compartmentalization and prevents vacuolar leakage/degradation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: BGLU23 protein localization/accumulation; ER-body formation module
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes sequestration and accumulation of BGLU23 in ER bodies; indirectly maintains effective glucosinolate hydrolysis capacity
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Compartmentation of glucosinolate-hydrolyzing β-glucosidase for single-cell chemical defense
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential for ER-body formation and for maintaining BGLU23 abundance required for glucosinolate-dependent defense
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Native promoter-driven expression examined in Arabidopsis seedlings; ER-body component in Brassicaceae
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: When expressed with BGLU23 in onion and tobacco, induces ER-body formation; in Arabidopsis nai2 mutant absence causes loss of ER bodies
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Seedling and root tissues; heterologous expression in onion and tobacco; woodlouse feeding assays
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Pro35S (constitutive) in onion/tobacco; native NAI2 promoter in Arabidopsis NAI2-GFP line
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous coexpression in onion and tobacco; nai2 mutant localization and immunoblot analysis; co-immunoprecipitation; mass spectrometry; woodlouse feeding assay
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables ER-body formation, sequesters and stabilizes BGLU23, and is required for full glucosinolate-based defense against woodlice
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NAI2 coexpression with BGLU23 is sufficient to induce ER bodies in non-Brassicaceae onion and tobacco cells. 2. NAI2 physically associates with BGLU23 by co-immunoprecipitation and proteomics. 3. Loss of NAI2 causes BGLU23 leakage into vacuoles and reduces BGLU23 protein abundance. 4. nai2 roots are more susceptible to woodlouse feeding, demonstrating loss of defense.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of NAI2 abolishes ER bodies and causes BGLU23 degradation; no additional growth penalty reported
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key component for reconstructing Brassicaceae-like single-cell chemical defense in heterologous crops; likely must be co-engineered with BGLU23
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although not a transcription factor, NAI2 is a decisive regulator of defense metabolism through compartmentation. Acquisition of NAI2 is proposed as a key evolutionary step in Brassicales single-cell defense.
